Reduced Crown: Less than 1/4 of the crown volume has been removed.
Weak or Yellow Foliage: The leaves appear to be somewhat smaller than normal or are somewhat pale in colour.
Dead or Broken Branch: The tree has at least one minor dead or broken branches or stubs originating from the main stem or a scaffold branch.
Lean: The tree has a slight lean (< 15° from vertical) but no evidence of soil cracking or mounding on the side away from the lean.
Poor Branch Attachment: There is a V-shaped union between a minor branch and the main stem but there is no evidence of included bark or failure.
Branch Scars: One or more major branches has scars with a combined width between 1/8 and 1/4 the circumference of the branch.
Trunk Scars: One or more minor scars (with a combined width 1/8 to 1/4 the circumference and no longer than 50 cm) are present on the stem.
Conks: One or more conks are present.
Rot or Cavity - Branch: An area of rot or an open cavity which is 1/8 to 1/4 the diameter of one or more major branches is present.
Rot or Cavity - Trunk: An area of rot or an open cavity which is 1/8 to 1/4 the diameter of the stem is present.
Confined Space: An obstruction exists which would eliminate root development in an area between 1/8 and 1/4 of the area within the dripline of the tree.
Crack: One minor crack extends into the wood of the stem.
Girdling Roots: There is evidence of girdling roots restricting the growth of the stem over 1/8 to 1/4 of the circumference of the stem.
Recent Trenching: Recent trenching may have caused root damage or loss in an area from 1/8 to 1/4 of that under the dripline of the tree.

Reduced Crown: Between 1/4 and 1/2 of the crown volume has been  removed.
Defoliation: From 1/4 to 1/2 of the crown has lost its leaves; The crown is moderately defoliated.
Weak or Yellow Foliage: The leaves are significantly smaller than what is normal or they are slightly chlorotic; the crown is significantly more transparent than typical for the species.
Dead or Broken Branch: The tree has one or more moderately sized dead or broken branches or stubs originating from the main stem or a scaffold branch.
Lean: The tree has a slight lean (< 15° from vertical) with some evidence of root mounding or soil cracking on the side of the tree away from the lean.
Poor Branch Attachment: There is a V-shaped union between a major branch and the main stem and there is evidence of included bark but the union still appears to be intact.
Branch Scars: One or more major branches have scars with a combined width between 1/4 and 1/2 the circumference of the branch.
Trunk Scars: One or more significant scars (with a combined width 1/4 to 1/2 the circumference or a minor scar longer than 50 cm) are present on the stem.
Conks: One or more conks are present.
Rot or Cavity - Branch: An area of rot or an open cavity which is 1/4 to 1/2 the diameter of one or more major branches is present.
Rot or Cavity - Trunk: An area of rot or an open cavity which is 1/4 to 1/2 the diameter of the stem is present.
Confined Space: An obstruction exists which would eliminate root development in an area 1/4 to 1/2 of the area within the dripline of the tree.
Crack: Two or more minor cracks occur on the stem.
Girdling Roots: There is evidence of girdling roots restricting the growth of the stem over 1/4 to 1/2 of the circumference of the stem.
Recent Trenching: Recent trenching may have caused root damage or loss in an area from 1/4 to 1/2 of that under the dripline of the tree.

Reduced Crown: More than 1/2 of the crown volume has been removed.
Unbalanced Crown: Its crown is severely asymmetrical to the point where it clearly places damaging stress on the main stem and/or the root system.
Defoliation: More than 1/2 of the crown has lost its leaves; the crown is severely defoliated.
Weak or Yellow Foliage: Leaves are dramatically smaller than normal and/or leaf colour is dramatically different; the tree appears to be in a serious state of decline.
Dead or Broken Branch: The tree has one or more large dead or broken branches or stubs originating from the main stem or a scaffold branch.
Lean: The tree has a serious lean (>15° from vertical) with some evidence of root mounding or soil cracking on the side of the tree away from the lean.
Poor Branch Attachment: There is a V-shaped union between a major branch and the main stem with evidence of included bark and/or the union is showing signs of failure.
Branch Scars: One or more major branch has scars with a combined width greater than 1/2 the circumference of the branch.
Trunk Scars: One or more major scars (with a combined width greater than 1/2 the circumference or a significant scar longer than 50 cm) are present on the stem.
Conks: One or more conks are present.
Rot or Cavity - Branch: An area of rot or an open cavity which is greater than 1/2 the diameter of one or more major branches is present.
Rot or Cavity - Trunk: An area of rot or an open cavity which is greater than 1/2 the diameter of the stem is present.
Confined Space: An obstruction exists which would eliminate root development in an area more than 1/2 of the area within the dripline of the tree.
Crack: One major crack (extending more than 1/2 the diameter of the stem) is present or one or more major cracks is in contact with another defect.
Girdling Roots: There is evidence of girdling roots restricting the growth of the stem over more than 1/2 of the circumference of the stem.
Recent Trenching: Recent trenching may have caused root damage or loss in an area more than 1/2 of that under the dripline of the tree.
